coredata.dc:title: Synergistic catalytic performance of Co–Fe/CQD nanocatalyst for rapid hydrogen generation through NaBH4 hydrolysis

Rezaei, Mehran
coredata.dc:description:
In this study, a bimetallic Co–Fe nanocatalyst supported on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is introduced as an effective catalyst for promoting hydrogen generation upon hydrolysis of sodium borohydride. Hydrothermal synthesis routes were used to produce CQDs support. All samples were subjected to analysis using XRD, UV–Vis, Raman, FTIR, FESEM, EDX, and nitrogen adsorption-desorption techniques. Benefiting from the higher surface area (51
m 2
g r
), pore volume (0.200
m L
g r
), and good dispersion of Co–Fe nanoparticles over the CQDs support, the CoFe/CQD spherical nanocatalysts with 5 wt.% iron content exhibited the highest catalytic activity of 20200 mL/min.gcat within only 1 min of reaction. The kinetics studies revealed a low activation energy of 48 kJ/mol over this optimum catalyst. In addition, it was found that the catalytic performance of this catalyst was decreased after 4 runs of recycling experiments. Catalyst degradation and leaching of the active phase were likely responsible for the decline in catalyst performance.

head.ce:title: Synergistic catalytic performance of Co–Fe/CQD nanocatalyst for rapid hydrogen generation through NaBH4 hydrolysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this study, a bimetallic Co–Fe nanocatalyst supported on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is introduced as an effective catalyst for promoting hydrogen generation upon hydrolysis of sodium borohydride. Hydrothermal synthesis routes were used to produce CQDs support. All samples were subjected to analysis using XRD, UV–Vis, Raman, FTIR, FESEM, EDX, and nitrogen adsorption-desorption techniques. Benefiting from the higher surface area (51
m 2
g r
), pore volume (0.200
m L
g r
), and good dispersion of Co–Fe nanoparticles over the CQDs support, the CoFe/CQD spherical nanocatalysts with 5 wt.% iron content exhibited the highest catalytic activity of 20200 mL/min.gcat within only 1 min of reaction. The kinetics studies revealed a low activation energy of 48 kJ/mol over this optimum catalyst. In addition, it was found that the catalytic performance of this catalyst was decreased after 4 runs of recycling experiments. Catalyst degradation and leaching of the active phase were likely responsible for the decline in catalyst performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen has recently attracted much attention as a clean energy carrier and a fossil energy alternative. Hydrogen is being considered as a replacement for fossil fuels due to several reasons. Firstly, it can be produced from different sources such as hydrocarbons, water, nuclear power, etc. [1]. Secondly, hydrogen is environmentally friendly as the only byproduct of its oxidation is water [2]. Thirdly, hydrogen can be stored in large quantities and transported over long distances, unlike electricity [3]. However, due to its lightweight, hydrogen gas requires a large storage volume. On the other hand, compressed hydrogen gas storage exhibits a low energy density, rendering it unsuitable for long-distance transportation applications [4]. Furthermore, the flammable nature of hydrogen during transportation poses another challenge that must be addressed. Hydrogen generation from formic acid, hydrazine, dimethylamine-borane, alcohols, and others attracted many researchers attention for many decades [5]. Hydrogen production by hydrolysis of chemically hydrogen storage materials is one of the simple and controllable methods by which hydrogen can be produced whenever needed without worrying about its storage and transportation. The most useful hydrogen storage material is sodium borohydride and the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride in the presence of a suitable catalyst has attracted a lot of attention. Sodium borohydride has advantages such as high gravimetric hydrogen content (10.8 wt.%), a safe structure, no toxicity, etc. [6]. Borohydride hydrolysis can serve as a viable source of hydrogen that can be readily supplied to Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells (PEMFC) to power electronic devices like vehicles, smartphones, tablets, and so on. There is much research that studied sodium borohydride hydrolysis for on-board vehicular and some other applications. Alpaydin et al. [7], in their study, compared a 700-bar compressed gas tank, as the hydrogen storage in fuel cell vehicles, and a sodium borohydride-based hydrogen storage system in terms of the range of the vehicle. The results showed that the NaBH4-based hydrogen storage system provided a 4.42% more range than the compressed gas tank. Kirk et al. [8], studied solid-state sodium borohydride in acid-mediated hydrolysis systems for on-board vehicular applications. Their proposed method afforded a high purity H2 storage density of 6.33 wt%, which could be extended to 10.4 wt.% considering 75% H2O recovery from fuel cells, while the achievable H2 storage densities in the system of the aqueous sodium borohydride system using metal catalysts were limited to between 2.2 and 3.2 wt.% owing to the use of excess H2O. Ekinci [9] studied the effect of feeding the hydrogen gas obtained in the NaBH4 hydrolysis process to a fuel cell, on its efficiency. Under the operational condition of temperature:40 °C, solution volume:10 mL, catalyst amount:25 mg, and resistance:10 Ohm, the results showed the average efficiency value according to the ideal voltage was 80%. Lensing [10], by using fuel cells instead of internal combustion engines, designed a hydrogen hybrid propulsion system fuelled by NaBH4. They studied the effectiveness of the system for a small naval vessel and used different configurations to estimate the highest energy density of the system. Finally, they conclude that NaBH4 provides a relatively safe option for hydrogen storage and production onboard vessels. To date, various catalysts like precious metals, metal oxides, non-noble metals, etc. have been used to boost hydrogen generation via hydrolysis reaction [11]. Due to the expensiveness of noble metals, the research was directed towards the use of non-noble metals. Among the studied catalysts, cobalt-based ones, while having a low price and availability, showed good catalytic activity, even close to noble metals. Cobalt oxide after being reduced by sodium borohydride will lead to the production of active Co–B compounds, which is a strong catalyst in the hydrolysis reaction [12]. However, one of the major problems is their tendency to aggregate, resulting in loss of catalytic activity [13]. As a result, approaches such as using catalytic support, using transition metals as promoters, etc. were raised. Among different supports, carbon-based ones are one of the best for achieving this goal due to their high porosity, low density, large specific surface area, etc. [14]. Bu et al. [15], synthesized Co nanoparticles supported on bagasse-derived porous carbon as a catalyst for the H2 generation from the hydrolysis of NaBH4. Using carbon as a support material, by inhibiting the agglomeration phenomenon of the composite catalyst, improved the catalytic activity. The high hydrogen generation rate of 11086.4 mL/min∙gCo and low activation energy of 31.25 kJ/mol have been obtained after supporting the catalyst. Sun et al. [16], supported Co–Ni nanoparticles on a carbon sphere catalyst materials which effectively increased the catalyst surface area and improved the distribution of metal nanoparticles on the catalyst surface. The results showed a high hydrogen generation rate of 6364 mL/min·gmetal by using this catalyst. Bukowska et al. [17], synthesized a Ni–CoP catalyst by electrodeposition and studied its performance towards hydrogen generation via hydrolysis of NaBH4. The catalyst was deposited in 3 steps on a carbon paper support. This catalyst showed a high amount of hydrogen that was generated in a short time, approximately 550 mL in 1 min. It also showed exceptional stability, retaining 54% of its initial activity after 300 h of a hydrolysis process. Luo et al. [18], studied Co3O4-carbon nanotube catalyst for accelerating hydrogen generation in the NaBH4 hydrolysis process. CNTs served as a support to prevent the aggregation of catalysts. To test the catalytic activity of this catalyst, the experiments of NaBH4 hydrolysis were performed under microwave irradiation. Biehler et al. [19], explored the synthesis of silver nanoparticles supported on fused carbon spheres (Ag-FCS) and then tested it for its ability to catalyze the NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction. Due to its high surface area, excellent stability, and efficient dispersion of Ag NPs, they offered this kind of carbon-based material for preventing active phase agglomeration. The results showed a low activation energy of 37 kJ/mol over this novel catalyst. In our previous study [20], our team aimed to achieve high catalytic activity by immobilizing cobalt nanomaterials on a Fe3O4@GO carbon-based carrier. As a result, we were able to achieve a maximum hydrogen generation rate of 6005 mL/min.gcat and an activation energy of 44.4 kJ/mol over this supported catalyst [20]. Carbon quantum dots are a renowned category of carbonaceous nanomaterials. Characterized by their outstanding biocompatibility, significant quantum yield, and robust absorption capabilities, these nanoparticles are viewed as highly promising for various applications, including biomedical applications, optoelectronic devices, catalytic processes, etc. [21]. To date, there hasn't been any research on the use of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a support in the NaBH4 hydrolysis process.

Research has indicated that another way to enhance the catalytic activity of Co-based catalysts and decrease their agglomeration is using a promoter besides the cobalt active phase. This is because promoters like transition metals transfer electrons to active Co sites, resulting in enhanced catalytic activity of Co sites. In addition, a suitable promoter can increase the active surface area and enhance metal dispersion [22]. Furthermore, adding promoter to the active phase by making a kind of bimetallic catalyst, can effect on electronic interactions and synergistic effect between metals and metal-support, as many studies reported [23]. Wang et al. [24] by introducing Fe to the cobalt nanocatalyst, observed that the maximum rate of hydrogen generation increased from 2773 mL/min.gcat for the Co catalyst to 4310 mL/min.gcat for the Co–Fe catalyst. Additionally, the activation energy of the Co–Fe nanocatalyst decreased to 29.09 kJ/mol, compared to 30.85 kJ/mol with Co. Patel et al. [25], to avoid agglomeration of Co–B particles, introduced various transition metals, Ni, Fe, Cu, Cr, Mo, and W. The results showed when Cr, W, and Mo were added to the Co–B catalyst, they increased the H2 generation rate by 3–4 times compared to the undoped catalyst. However, Ni and Fe had a small effect on the catalytic performance of the Co–B catalyst. In another study, Hansu [26] discovered that by adding tungsten to the Ru structure, the catalyst activity of ruthenium-based supported multi-walled carbon nanotube has been significantly increased. This is due to the change in the electronic structure of the Ru. The hydrolysis results over this catalyst showed a high hydrogen generation rate of 95841 mL/min∙gcat and a low activation energy of 16.32 kJ/mol. So, using an appropriate promoter can be as beneficial as utilizing support in enhancing the catalytic activity of Co-based catalysts.

After reviewing various studies, we found that so far there hasn't been any research conducted on the use of carbon quantum dots as a support in the process of hydrogen generation through the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride. To fill this gap, first, we synthesized CQDs using a hydrothermal route as a carbon-based carrier and then loaded cobalt as an active phase and iron as a promoter through the impregnation-chemical reduction method. By doing so, we aim to take advantage of two key solutions that help to reduce the catalyst's agglomeration and increase its activity: supporting the active phase and using the promoter alongside the active phase. Accordingly, we investigated the effect of these newly developed catalysts on accelerating hydrogen generation via the sodium borohydride hydrolysis process and other kinetic aspects.

Starting materials for the catalyst synthesis were Cobalt (II) nitrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), Nickel(II) nitrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), Citric acid (C6H8O7) and urea (CO(NH2)2). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were also used for the hydrolysis reactions. all of the reagents were supplied from Merck company and used without further purification.

Carbon quantum dots were synthesized using the hydrothermal method by following the procedure described by Qiu et al. [27]. The synthesis utilized environmentally friendly precursors and was completed in a short time. Initially, 0.42 g of citric acid (as a carbon source) and 0.36 g of urea were mixed in 10 ml of distilled water for 10 min to produce a clear solution. The solution was then placed in an autoclave and heated to 160 °C for 4 h. After this, the solution inside the Teflon lined autoclave was transferred to a beaker and placed in an oven at 80 °C for 24 h to evaporate the water. This process resulted in a solid brown powder, which is a carbon quantum dot. To load the metal with a total content of 30% by weight on this carrier, the chemical reduction method using sodium borohydride as a reducing agent was employed. To do this, cobalt nitrate, iron nitrate, and 0.5 g of quantum dots were dissolved in 50 cc of deionized water and stirred for 30 min. Then, 20 ml of a 0.23 M solution of sodium borohydride was added to the above aqueous solution. After 30 min of mixing, the product was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 15 min and washed completely with deionized water. This centrifugation and washing process was repeated 3 times. Finally, the washed solid material was dried at 80 °C for 12 h. The resulting catalysts were named CoFe(X–Y)/CQD, where X represents the weight percent of cobalt metal and Y represents the weight percentage of iron metal.

To evaluate the catalytic performance of synthetic nanostructures, 15 mg of catalyst was poured into a balloon and the balloon was placed in a water bath with a constant temperature of 35 °C. Then 3 mL aqueous solution containing 5 wt.% sodium borohydride and 4 wt.% of sodium hydroxide were prepared. The solution was then injected into a balloon using a syringe, which quickly started the production of hydrogen. The volume of hydrogen produced from the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride was measured by calculating the amount of water displaced in an Erlenmeyer flask and its transfer to a graduated cylinder. Each experiment was repeated three times, and the average values were reported. Once the reaction was complete, the catalyst was separated from the solution by centrifugation, washed several times with distilled water, and then reused after being dried in the oven. Fig. S1 shows an experimental setup used for generating H2 from hydrolysis of NaBH4.

The physicochemical properties of as-prepared catalysts were studied by various characterization techniques. The crystalline structure of synthesized samples was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. This analysis was executed at room temperature using a DRON-8 Russian instrument at a Bragg angle of 2θ = 5–80°. Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) was done by Chinese UV-2100 scanning spectrophotometer. The morphology of the products was characterized by a TESCAN-MIRA3 scanning field electron microscopy (FESEM) analyzer. The Raman Takram P50C0R10 spectroscopy instrument investigated the structural features of the as-prepared samples. The functional groups of the synthesized catalysts were analyzed by American PerkinElmer Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Surface area and other pore characteristic was determined on the Japanese Belsorp mini II instrument using Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method.

UV–Visible spectral analysis (UV–Vis) was conducted for the as-prepared CQDs in the 200–800 nm range. The absorption spectrum of the CQDs is depicted in Fig. S2. According to previous studies, the strong absorption peak at 326 nm is attributed to the n
→ P
*
electronic transition of the CO band [28]. The X-ray diffraction pattern of carbon quantum dots is depicted in Fig. 1 . The main characteristic of carbon quantum dots is a wide peak centered at 2θ = 20–30°. The presence of this broad peak confirms the poor crystalline structure of carbon quantum dots [29]. A comparison of spectrums reveals that the addition of cobalt and iron did not alter the structure of carbon quantum dots. Moreover, the introduction of cobalt and iron did not result in any peaks related to these two metals, due to their large dispersion. This result may indicate the formation of the amorphous structure of these metal from metal ions during the reduction process in the presence of sodium borohydride, as reported in several studies [30].

Fig. 2 shows the microscopic images from the surface of the synthesized samples. The investigations carried out by the scanning electron microscope showed that the synthesized carbon quantum dot has a sheet structure. It is evident from the images that the introduction of cobalt resulted in the mounting of spherical cobalt nanoparticles on the carbon quantum dot sheet. Incorporating low amounts of iron results in a more uniform distribution of iron and cobalt nanoparticles on the support. When the iron quantity increases, it causes nanoparticles to agglomerate. Comparing the images, we can see that the most evenly distributed nanoparticles are found on a CoFe(95-5)/CQD sample. Lastly, the EDX mapping of CoFe(95-5)/CQD in Fig. 2 confirms that its constituent elements, carbon, oxygen, cobalt, and iron, are uniformly dispersed and not accumulated in any particular area.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption measurements of synthesized catalysts and their results are reported in Table 1 . The surface area of a catalyst is crucial to determine the concentration and distribution of active sites. A higher surface area leads to more active sites being available for reactive molecules [31]. The addition of cobalt to the bare CQDs initially increased the surface area and pore volume, which was further enhanced by the combination of iron and cobalt. The introduction of cobalt and iron could increase the surface area due to chemical or physical changes in the support. These changes may include oxidation, reduction, the creation of additional surface roughness, defects, active sites, gasification, etc. This could lead to the creation of new pores or the enlargement of existing ones. In addition, when Co and Fe nanoparticles are loaded onto the carrier, they can interact with the surface of the support through various mechanisms such as physical adsorption, chemical bonding, or diffusion into the support matrix. These interactions can lead to the formation of a new phase or structure at the interface between nanoparticles and the support so, increasing the pore characteristic. However, it appears that there is a trade-off between the content of metal and the surface area value because it has been observed that adding iron to the CoFe(95-5)/CQD, more than 5 wt.%, reduced the surface area and pore volume, probably due to the catalysts pore blocking. The CoFe(95-5)/CQD sample had the greatest specific surface area and pore volume.

The pore size influences the surface area available for catalytic reactions. A catalyst with smaller pores may have a higher surface area, which can increase the number of active sites available for the reaction to occur [32]. However, a catalyst with a smaller pore size, due to the restricted access to its active sites, hinders the diffusion of reactants and products, leading to lower reaction rates and they are more susceptible to deactivation [33]. So, the trade-off between pore characteristic parameters including surface area, pore volume, and pore size makes the final result. Nevertheless, it is expected that the CoFe(95-5)/CQD nanocatalyst with the largest surface area and pore volume will exhibit higher activity and better performance in the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride.

Fig. 3 presents nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms of all synthesized samples. The isotherm obtained for all the prepared samples indicated a type III isotherm. This shows that there is no identifiable monolayer formation, and interactions between adsorbates are stronger than those between adsorbates and adsorbents [34]. Additionally, all supported catalysts showed a type H3 hysteresis loop, indicating the presence of macropores in the structure of these catalysts. The pore size distribution diagram, in the inset of Fig. 3, confirms the existence of these pores with different dimensions and shows that the pore size of this catalyst ranges from about 1 nm to 70 nm on average.

FT-IR spectroscopy, in the range of 450–4400 cm−1, was used to identify the components and functional groups present on the surface of synthesized CQDs and CoFe(95-5)/CQD samples. The FTIR spectra of samples, in Fig. 4 , reveal a peak at 670 cm−1, corresponding to the C–OC vibrations [35]. The peak at 1378 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of –COOH and CH2. The peak at 2368 cm−1 is associated with the bending vibration of the CC group [36]. For the samples, the physical absorption of water on the product was indicated by the observation of the O–H stretching vibration absorption peak at 3440 cm−1 wavelengths. Upon comparing the images, it was observed that there were no new significant peaks detected after the introduction of cobalt and iron. Some small peaks at positions 800-1200 cm−1 and 3800-4200 cm−1 are most likely due to the absorption of the environmental impurities. Additionally, the introduction of cobalt and iron did not cause any damage to the catalytic support.

The structural features of the as-prepared CQDs were determined using Raman spectra and the result is depicted in Fig. S3. Two predominant peaks at 1361 and 1573 cm−1 are assigned to the disordered D and crystalline G bands, respectively. The D band corresponds to disordered Sp2 carbon atoms, while the G band corresponds to the stretching vibration mode E2g of crystalline graphite carbon atoms [37]. Based on the Raman spectra, it is evident that the G band is more prominent than the D band, with an intensity ratio of 1.45. This suggests that the synthesized CQDs exhibit a high degree of graphitization and relatively low levels of defects or disorders in the graphitized structure [38]. In addition, the ratio of intensities of the D and G bands (ID/IG) being less than 2 reveals the nanocrystalline nature of the CQDs [39].

To compare the performance of catalysts in the sodium borohydride hydrolysis process, one of the important parameters is the hydrogen generation rate (HGR). This parameter is defined as milliliters of hydrogen generated per minute, per gram of catalyst. In this study, catalysts were compared by measuring the maximum rate of hydrogen generation (HGRmax), as reported in the literature [40]. The measurements were taken at 35 °C using 15 mg of catalyst and 3 mL of a solution consisting of 5 wt.% NaBH4 and 4 wt.% NaOH.

A graph comparing the hydrogen generated over time using different catalysts is shown in Fig. 5 . As can be seen, the carbon quantum dots support alone produced very little hydrogen, indicating that it does not have a catalytic role. However, when cobalt was loaded as an active phase on the CQDs carrier, the hydrogen generation rate increased significantly. After adding iron metal as a promoter, the maximum rate of hydrogen generation increased as shown in the interior table of Fig. 5. Among bimetallic nanoparticles, the CoFe(95-5)/CQD catalyst had the highest HGRmax, 20200 mL/min.g, compared to the other catalysts under similar conditions. This particular catalyst was able to release 303 mL of hydrogen only in 1 min. According to the mechanism proposed in our previous study [31], in this process, first the reactants, BH4 − and H2O, are adsorbed on the catalyst. Next, BH4 − ions undergo chemisorption on the catalyst surface. The active catalytic sites break the B–H bond in the NaBH4 molecule, producing intermediate species CoBH3- and CoH. These intermediates react with H2O (HOH) to generate the BH3(OH)- ion and H2 and degeneration of the catalyst surface. By transferring H from BH3(OH)- ion to an unoccupied cobalt atom, this cycle continued as BH3(OH)- →BH2(OH)2 -→ BH(OH)3 -→ B(OH)4 -. So, hydrogen is released at each step and finally, 4H2 is produced (NaBH4 + 4H2O → NaB(OH)4 + 4H2). In the proposed mechanism, the rate-determining step is often considered breaking a strong bond between NaBH4 and H2O. The other steps in the process are usually faster and do not significantly affect the overall rate of the reaction. On the other side, the active sites responsible for hydrolysis reaction are electron-rich cobalt nanoparticles and bimetallic catalysts have the advantage of possessing electron transfer properties [41]. As mentioned in the literature, iron is one of the metals that possess this ability and can transfer electrons from iron to cobalt so, when iron is added, the electron density of the cobalt active sites increases [24]. As a result, bimetallic CoFe/CQD samples by electron donating properties can produce more hydrogen in a shorter time, compare with monometallic Co/CQD. Therefore, to investigate the effect of operating conditions on the performance of the catalyst, the sample CoFe(95-5)/CQD with the highest HGRmax was subjected to further investigations as the optimum sample.

Fig. 6 investigates the impact of different concentrations of sodium borohydride (3, 5, 7, and 9 wt.% relative to the weight of the solvent) on the hydrogen generation volume. The maximum HGRs achieved, as can be seen, increased with the increment of the concentration of sodium borohydride from 3 wt.% to 5 wt.%. This is because a higher concentration of borohydride ions contacts more with the active sites on the catalyst surface. However, increasing the concentration of sodium borohydride beyond 5 wt.% leads to a decrease in the maximum rate of hydrogen generation. This is due to the increased viscosity of the solution, which disrupts the hydrolysis process [42]. Moreover, exceeding the optimal concentration limits the solubility of NaBO2 by-product in water, leading to precipitation that hinders accessibility to the catalyst's active site [43].

Fig. 7 investigates the effect of different concentrations of sodium hydroxide (2, 3, 4, and 5 wt.% relative to the weight of the solvent) on the hydrogen generation volume. The maximum hydrogen generation rate (HGRmax) values obtained for 2, 3, 4, and 5 wt.% NaOH were 9733, 15800, 20200, and 17606 mL/min.gcat, respectively. As can be seen, the maximum HGR increased with the increment of the concentration of NaOH from 2 to 4 wt.%. However, upon further increasing the sodium hydroxide concentration, a reverse trend in the hydrogen generation profile was observed. When the concentration of the solution exceeds the optimal value, which is 4 wt.%, it causes an increase in the solution's viscosity and alkalinity. This, in turn, reduces the water activity which makes it difficult for the hydrolysis reaction to occur. Additionally, if too much NaOH is used, it can decrease the solubility of NaBO2 byproducts. This leads to the precipitation of these species on the surface of the catalyst and can block the active sites [44].

Fig. 8 illustrates the impact of the amount of catalyst, ranging from 10 to 25 mg, on its performance in the sodium borohydride hydrolysis process. The maximum hydrogen generation rate (HGRmax) values obtained for 10, 15, 20, and 25 mg of catalyst were 11333, 20200, 28000, and 11666 mL/min.gcat, respectively. It can be observed that the maximum HGR increased as the amount of catalyst was increased from 10 to 20 mg. However, upon the addition of catalyst quantity from 20 to 25 mg, the maximum HGR decreased. This occurs because an increase in the amount of catalyst exposes more active sites to sodium borohydride initially. These active sites cause the breaking of the B–H bond in the sodium borohydride molecule and the formation of BH3 molecules, which can further react with water to produce more hydrogen [45]. In addition, intermediate species formed on active catalyst sites such as B(OH)3, B(OH)4 -, and HBO2, may release additional hydrogen upon further hydrolysis reaction. However, with too much catalyst, the concentration of the reaction solution increases, leading to mass transfer limitations and a decrease in catalyst activity.

The graph in Fig. 9 illustrates how temperature affects hydrogen generation during the hydrolysis process of sodium borohydride via a CoFe(95-5)/CQD catalyst. The test was conducted between 25 and 40 °C, and the maximum HGR results were recorded at each temperature. The highest HGR values at 25 °C, 30 °C, 35 °C, and 40 °C were 10133, 14000, 20200, and 26000 mL/min.gcat, respectively. So, as the temperature increased from 25 to 40 °C, the maximum HGR increased by 157%. This can be attributed to a decrease in solution viscosity so an increase in the rate of BH4 − ions penetration into active sites at higher temperatures [46,47]. This finding aligns with the results obtained in other studies [48].

According to numerous literature reports, the process of hydrolyzing NaBH4 follows zero-order kinetics [49]. As an important kinetic parameter, activation energy (Ea) for NaBH4 hydrolysis over CoFe(95-5)/CQD Catalyst is determined using the Arrhenius equation and its slope.

Here, r is the hydrogen generation rate (mL/min.gcat), k0 is the pre-exponential factor, Ea is activation energy (kJ/mol), T is the temperature of reaction (K), and R is the ideal gas constant (8.314 J/mol.K). The values of the hydrogen generation rate and the rate constant at different temperatures are calculated from the slope of the initial linear part of each diagram. The slope of the Arrhenius plot, Fig. 10 a, was used to calculate the activation energy. By this, Ea was found to be 48 kJ/mol. The low activation energy may be due to the uniform dispersion of cobalt and iron nanoparticles on the CQDs carrier which prevents their accumulation.

Further, to determine the thermodynamic parameters of the reaction like activation enthalpy, activation entropy, and Gibbs free energy, we used again the temperature effect data presented in Fig. 10 and converted them into Erying plots as below:

Where K is the hydrogen generation rate, ΔH: activation enthalpy, ΔS: activation entropy, kB: Boltzmann's constant, h: Planck's constant, R: ideal gas constant, and T is the reaction temperature. By plotting the Ln K/T diagram against 1/T and analyzing the resulting graph's slope and intercept, the enthalpy and entropy of activation were determined as 45.5 kJ/mol and −17.21 J/K, respectively. The positive activation enthalpy value shows that forming an active complex from the primary reactants is an endothermic process. Meanwhile, the negative activation entropy value suggests that the formation of the active complex involves a greater degree of order than the reactants. This observation supports the Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism [50]. Moreover, by using the average temperature of 305.6 K and applying the formula ΔG = ΔH-TΔS, we have determined the Gibbs free energy to be 50.75 kJ/mol. This value indicates that the formation of the complex resulting from the reaction between the raw materials is a non-spontaneous process.

The reusability of the catalyst is an important feature of a heterogeneous catalyst as it determines the commercial applicability of a catalyst system. To evaluate the potential of the catalyst in generating hydrogen from NaBH4 hydrolysis, following each test, the sample was retrieved from the reaction medium, washed thoroughly, dried in the oven, and reused. As depicted in Fig. 11 , the rate of hydrogen generation was found to decrease as the number of cycles increased, with the most significant decrease observed in the third cycle. When the cycle number reaches 4, the H2 generation rate is still as high as 7876 mL/min.g and the activity maintains 70 % of the initial activity, suggesting that the CoFe/CQDs catalyst possesses good activity even in the case of multi-usage and so has relatively good reusability.

In addition, a detailed comparison of the reusability of this catalyst with other Co-based catalysts reported in the literature is presented in Table 2 . From the table, it appears that this catalyst exhibits relatively good stability compared to others.

The decline in catalyst activity could be due to various reasons. To investigate these reasons, FESEM and EDX analyses were conducted on the spent samples. The FESEM of the fresh and spent catalysts is depicted in Fig. 12 a,b The result shows that the structure of the used sample is completely different from the fresh one which suggests the degradation of the CoFe(95-5)/CQD structures. The fresh sample displayed spherical particles with a mean diameter of 80–100 nm, while the spent catalyst appeared agglomerated and amorphous structure. Fig. 12c,d shows the EDX patterns of both the fresh and spent CoFe(95-5)/CQD catalyst. As seen from the figure, the intensity of peaks attributed to cobalt, and iron decreased after four cycles of use, which is possibly due to catalyst washing. Moreover, the catalytic components are probably dissolved in the alkaline environment of sodium borohydride.

Table 3 compares the hydrogen generation potential of different carbon-based catalysts based on their hydrogen generation rate and activation energy. From this, it is obvious that the obtained results in the current study are comparable with the reported results in the literature. Results show our catalyst's superiority in comparison to the reported efforts in the literature in terms of HGRmax and activation energy.

The hydrogen produced by NaBH4 hydrolysis can widely be used as a reactant or reducing agent in the chemical industry, such as hydrogenation reactions or ammonia production. In addition, they can be used as a propellant in rockets and spacecraft, enabling long-duration space missions and exploration of distant planets. Currently, the most promising application of this process is to produce an on-demand supply of hydrogen for fuel cells, enabling clean and efficient power generation for hydrogen fuel vehicles, and portable electronic devices like smartphones, tablets, etc. In the introduction of this study, various research on on-board vehicular and other applications of NaBH4 hydrolysis are mentioned, demonstrating significant progress in this field and the promising future of sodium borohydride hydrolysis technology. However One of the major obstacles in using this technology is the fact that the anode of fuel cells works in an acidic environment, whereas an alkaline environment of this process and the produced vapors can negatively affect the performance of these cells [72]. Another main problem with this process is the high cost involved with the synthesis of sodium borohydride. However, there are new and more affordable methods for producing NaBH4, as well as the capability to recycle metaborate, which is a byproduct of the process, for the synthesis of sodium borohydride. These solutions could significantly reduce the overall cost of the process [73]. At the same time, researchers are striving to overcome these challenges and bring them closer to commercialization.

The study investigates the potential of a new structure catalyst, Co–Fe CQDs material, for hydrogen generation via the hydrolysis of NaBH4. The hydrolysis results showed that the maximum hydrogen generation rate using the bimetallic CoFe(95-5)/CQD catalyst was about three times higher than that achieved using the cobalt-based catalyst. Among different CoFe/CQD samples with varying Fe content, the sample CoFe(95-5)/CQD with the 5 wt.% Fe content showed the highest HGR, 20200 mL/min.g, and low activation energy of 48 kJ/mol. The stability of the prepared sample was also investigated through 4 cycles of recycling experiments, revealing a slight reduction in catalyst performance after each cycle. After a thorough investigation of the NaBH4 hydrolysis over this catalyst and its promising results, it is believed that this process can provide hydrogen for the fuel cell and other industrial applications. However, this technology is still at the initial development level and to achieve a practical level of utilization, it should include some aspects in its future studies, including the cost of NaBH4 production, alkaline impurities removal, scale-up, and some other engineering aspects.
